input_args.parameters_json_dump:
'{"abstract": "Data from: Local adaptation of trees at the range margins impact range shifts in the face of climate change The ability of tree species to track their climatic niche at rates comparable to global warming is concerning, particularly if they are constrained by local adaptation. If a species is locally adapted at its range margin it could be beneficial for range expansion as it ensures that the genotypes colonizing new areas are the fittest because environmental conditions are more similar to the current ones. In trees, local adaptation can slow range expansion when climate change happens much faster than their ability to migrate. We investigate experimentally a series of factors thought to constrain the seedling phase at the leading edge of the distribution of a dominant tree species, sugar maple (Acer saccharum Marshall.). We established a seed transplant experiment using six provenances, representing the latitudinal species range, and transplanted them to 12 sites within, at, and beyond the current northern species range margin. Northern provenances currently provide the best opportunity for establishment beyond the actual range, where climatic conditions are more similar than those of the warmer central or southern portions of the species range. While establishment was highest within the species range, survival rates were comparable to those at the range margin and beyond, regardless of provenance. We also find that the local climate is the most influential factor for early seedling establishment and survival; however, a lack of suitable microsites also significantly constrained recruitment. Our study highlights the complex interaction between provenance, climate, and microsite conditions that are required to ensure successful seedling recruitment. While sugar maple is currently displaying evidence for local adaptation to facilitate range shifts, it could risk maladaptation in the future if the local climate warms beyond threshold required to ensure seed germination and a lack of favourable microsite conditions beyond the range.
plot
This .csv file includes the raw data for the plot level assessment. The following information is included:_x000D_\\n_x000D_\\nsite: Transplant site _x000D_\\nmoss: % cover of moss_x000D_\\nleaf: % cover of deciduous leaves_x000D_\\nneedles: % cover of evergreen needles_x000D_\\nrock: % cover of rock_x000D_\\nsoft: % cover of soft or decomposed wood_x000D_\\nhard: % cover of hard wood_x000D_\\nexposed: % cover of exposed soil or other_x000D_\\npH: soil pH
seedweight
This .csv file includes the raw data used for the analysis in this experiment exploring seed performance. Below is _x000D_\\na description of each of the variables used _x000D_\\n_x000D_\\norigin: The seed origin or provenance from where the seed was collected. There were 6 different seed origins that were used in this study (N2: Rivi\\u00e8re-du-Loup, N1: Montmagny, C2: Sherbrooke, C1: Pennsylvania, S2: Kentucky, and S1: Tennessee). Weight: Mass (in grams) of seed lot (150 seeds).
transplant
This .csv file includes the raw data used for the analysis in this experiment that explored the macroscale effect by bioclimatic zone (temperate, mizedwood, and boreal) and seed origin or provenance._x000D_\\nThe following information is provided:_x000D_\\n_x000D_\\nstand: The dominant stand type where the seeds were transplanted (boreal, mixed, and temperate). _x000D_\\nsite: The transplant site where the seeds were transplanted. _x000D_\\norigin: The seed origin or provenance from where the seed was collected. There were 6 different seed origins used in this study _x000D_\\n(N2: Rivi\\u00e8re-du-Loup, N1: Montmagny, C2: Sherbrooke, C1: Pennsylvania, S2: Kentucky, and S1: Tennessee)._x000D_\\nfirst: The number of first year seedlings (count data)_x000D_\\nsecond: The number of second year seedlings (count data)_x000D_\\nsurvival: The proportion of first year seedlings surviving to the second year (second/first=survival).
